title: How to compute read coverage
text: “read coverage”, mean number reads cover given genomic position. Computing read coverage generally consists computing coverage position genome. can done coverage function GenomicAlignments. illustration, use data pasillaBamSubset data package. Single-end reads can read readGAlignments function GenomicAlignments. coverage can calculated using coverage function GenomicAlignments: can extract coverage specific chromosome: can also perform calculations returned object, e.g. get average maximum coverage chromosome 4: can use slice function find genomic regions coverage greater equal given threshold. weight given region can defined number aligned nucleotides belong region (.e. area coverage curve). can obtained sum: Note coverage slice generic functions methods different types objects. See ?coverage ?slice information.

title: How to compute sequence composition for genomic regions
text: Mammalian genomes contain regulatory regions high content CpG dinucleotides, called CpG islands, side effect indirectly caused methylation cytosines CpG contexts. Many transcripts contain CpG island promoter region, near start transcript. HowTo, identify transcripts analyzing CpG composition promoter sequences. first obtain coordinates promoter regions TxDb.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg38.knownGene package, using promoters function GenomicFeatures package: , promoter regions defined 2000 base pair regions centered transcript start sites (5’-end transcript). warning message caused start sites near boundary chromosome, promoter region extend beyond beginning end. calling trim GRanges object, regions truncated limits chromosomes, resulting promoters shorter 2000 base pairs: may useful now subset extracted promoters, example retain promoters autosomes, single promoter per gene. , remove redundant promoters resulting transcripts identical start sites: Next, extract sequences promoters reference genome assembly BSgenome.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg38 package: Finally, calculate sequence features: percent G C bases, ratio observed expected CpG dinucleotides (expected frequency dinucleotide product frequencies two mononucleotides). obtain mono- dinucleotide frequencies sequences using oligonucleotideFrequency function Biostrings package: Finally, can look distribution sequence features. percentage G+C bases promoters spans broad range 30% 70%:  Defining CpG island promoters using distribution hard, much easier using observed expected ratio CpGs, shows much clearer bimodal structure:  Interestingly, almost ratios 1.0, indicating CpG islands fact enriched CpG dinucleotides, rather lose lower rate genomic regions. Plotting ratio versus GC percentage furthermore reveals CpG island promoters also slightly GC-richer:

title: How to extract promoter sequences
text: start loading required packages. , get example data set. , use data model plant Arabidopsis thaliana. start obtaining genome sequences gene annotation Ensembl Plants. Working model non-model organisms , ’re obtaining data Ensembl Plants explicitly show can done organism. However, ’re lucky enough work model organism (e.g. human, mouse, etc), can find curated genome sequences gene annotation AnnotationHub (see, instance, HowTo article). Genome sequences stored DNAStringSet objects, gene annotation (.e., start end coordinates gene) stored GRanges objects. objects look like: Note GRanges object contains ranges multiple types features, including genes, mRNAs, exons, even entire chromosomes. Since ’re interested promoter regions, subset annotation keep ‘gene’ ranges. Next, can use promoters() function GenomicRanges extract genomic coordinates promoters. default, promoters start 2000 bp upstream transcription start site (TSS), end 200 bp downstream TSS, can modify positions parameters upstream downstream promoters(). example, transcription factor families plants (e.g. HSF C2C2-GATA) bind exclusively regions downstream TSS, contrary common view TFBS located upstream TSSs. Importantly, gene multiple TSSs (e.g., different transcripts starting different positions), upstream TSS used, cleverly chosen ‘representative’ ‘canonical’ transcript. Alternatively, want extract promoters transcript separately, can subset GRanges object extract ranges type ‘transcript’, use promoters() demonstrated . Note GFF3 files databases contain ‘transcript’ type. cases, likely find transcript-level ranges rows type ‘mRNA’. case Ensembl Plants, obtained example data. extract promoters . thaliana transcript, ’d execute: Extracting promoter regions TxDb objects TxDb object transcript coordinates metadata, can extract promoter regions transcript promoters() function GenomicFeatures package. Note function names , GenomicFeatures::promoters() takes TxDb objects input, GenomicRanges::promoters() takes GRanges objects input. Finally, can extract sequences given genome coordinates getSeq() function BSgenome.

what: How to get the exon and intron sequences of a given gene
title: How to get the exon and intron sequences of a given gene
text: exon intron sequences gene essentially DNA sequences introns exons known transcripts gene. first task, therefore, identify transcripts associated gene interest. example gene human TRAK2, involved regulation endosome--lysosome trafficking membrane cargo. Entrez gene id ‘66008’. TxDb.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg38.knownGene data package contains gene models corresponding UCSC ‘Known Genes’ track. transcript ranges genes gene model can extracted transcriptsBy function GenomicFeatures package. returned named GRangesList object containing transcripts grouped gene. order keep transcripts TRAK2 gene subset GRangesList object using [[ operator. trak2_txs GRanges object one range per transcript TRAK2 gene. transcript names stored tx_name metadata column. need subset extracted intron exon regions: exon intron genomic ranges transcripts gene model can extracted exonsBy intronsByTranscript functions, respectively. functions return GRangesList object. keep exon intron transcripts TRAK2 gene subsetting GRangesList object TRAK2 transcript names. Extract exon regions: … intron regions: Next want DNA sequences exons introns. getSeq function Biostrings package can used query BSgenome object set genomic ranges retrieve corresponding DNA sequences. Extract exon sequences: … intron sequences: list element DNAStringSet object, e.g.: sequence corresponds single intron.
code: trak2 <- "66008" suppressPackageStartupMessages({     library(TxDb.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg38.knownGene) }) txdb <- TxDb.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg38.knownGene suppressPackageStartupMessages({     library(GenomicFeatures) }) (trak2_txs <- transcriptsBy(txdb, by = "gene")[[trak2]]) #> GRanges object with 5 ranges and 2 metadata columns: #>       seqnames              ranges strand |     tx_id           tx_name #>          <Rle>           <IRanges>  <Rle> | <integer>       <character> #>   [1]     chr2 201377207-201451458      - |     61824 ENST00000451703.5 #>   [5]     chr2 201420417-201433554      - |     61825 ENST00000440597.1 #>   ------- #>   seqinfo: 711 sequences (1 circular) from hg38 genome (trak2_tx_names <- mcols(trak2_txs)$tx_name) #> [1] "ENST00000332624.8" "ENST00000430254.1" "ENST00000486291.1" #> [4] "ENST00000451703.5" "ENST00000440597.1" trak2_exbytx <- exonsBy(txdb, "tx", use.names = TRUE)[trak2_tx_names] elementNROWS(trak2_exbytx) #> ENST00000332624.8 ENST00000430254.1 ENST00000486291.1 ENST00000451703.5  #>                16                 8                 2                 3  #> ENST00000440597.1  #>                 2 trak2_inbytx <- intronsByTranscript(txdb, use.names = TRUE)[trak2_tx_names] elementNROWS(trak2_inbytx) #> ENST00000332624.8 ENST00000430254.1 ENST00000486291.1 ENST00000451703.5  #>                15                 7                 1                 2  #> ENST00000440597.1  #>                 1 suppressPackageStartupMessages({     library(BSgenome.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg38) }) (trak2_ex_seqs <- getSeq(Hsapiens, trak2_exbytx)) #> DNAStringSetList of length 5 #> [["ENST00000332624.8"]] GGCAGTGGGAGCAGCGGCAGCAGCTTCGGCTGCTGCTTTCAGGCTGCCGCTGC... #> [["ENST00000430254.1"]] GTGAAGTCGCCCGCCTGTCCCTGCCACGCCCGGGCGGTTGCTGGCAGTGGGAG... #> [["ENST00000486291.1"]] ATTTGGTATTTTAACAGAGGGATCGTGATCTGGAACTCGCTGCTCGAATTGGA... #> [["ENST00000451703.5"]] TGGGAGCAGCGGCAGCAGCTTCGGCTGCTGCTTTCAGGCTGCCGCTGCATTAG... #> [["ENST00000440597.1"]] GGAAATACTGACCCCATAAACCTAAATCTCACACACGGTTTTGGACGTTTAAA... what: How to read a BAM file in chunks
title: How to read a BAM file in chunks
text: large BAM file can iterated chunks, order reduce memory usage, setting yieldSize BamFile object. illustration, use data pasillaBamSubset data package. Iteration BAM file requires file opened, repeatedly queried inside loop, closed. Repeated calls GenomicAlignments::readGAlignments without opening file first result 100000 records returned time (yieldSize 100000). example, let’s calculate coverage bam file .
code: suppressPackageStartupMessages({     library(pasillaBamSubset)     library(Rsamtools) }) # Path to a bam file with single-end reads (un1 <- untreated1_chr4()) #> [1] "/Users/runner/work/_temp/Library/pasillaBamSubset/extdata/untreated1_chr4.bam" bf <- BamFile(un1, yieldSize = 100000) suppressPackageStartupMessages({     library(GenomicAlignments) }) open(bf) cvg <- NULL repeat {     chunk <- readGAlignments(bf)     if (length(chunk) == 0L) {         break     }     chunk_cvg <- coverage(chunk)     if (is.null(cvg)) {         cvg <- chunk_cvg     } else {         cvg <- cvg + chunk_cvg     } } close(bf) cvg #> RleList of length 8 #> $chr2L #> integer-Rle of length 23011544 with 1 run #>   Lengths: 23011544 #>   Values :        0 #>  #> $chr2R #> integer-Rle of length 21146708 with 1 run #>   Lengths: 21146708 #>   Values :        0 #>  #> $chr3L #> integer-Rle of length 24543557 with 1 run #>   Lengths: 24543557 #>   Values :        0 #>  #> $chr3R #> integer-Rle of length 27905053 with 1 run #>   Lengths: 27905053 #>   Values :        0 #>  #> $chr4 #> integer-Rle of length 1351857 with 122061 runs #>   Lengths:  891   27    5   12   13   45    5 ...    3  106   75 1600   75 1659 #>   Values :    0    1    2    3    4    5    4 ...    6    0    1    0    1    0 #>  #> ... #> <3 more elements>

what: How to read gene sets from GMT files
title: How to read gene sets from GMT files
text: package GSEABase provides object classes methods represent manipulate gene sets, including reading GMT files function getGmt(). first example read GMT file MSigDB database: Next filename GMT file, provided argument geneIdType=SymbolIdentifier(). argument adds necessary metadata later allows software figure kind gene identifiers used collection gene sets, attempt mapping type identifiers, necessary. argument optional, always try provide . output getGmt() function GeneSetCollection object, pretty much behaves like list object many Bioconductor packages analysing gene sets may expect kind object input. However, can coerce regular list object function geneIds(): definition, GMT files unique gene set identifiers attempt read getGmt() malformed GMT file duplicated identifiers prompt error: still want read contents GMT file, download edit lines duplicated identifiers. alternative use function readGMT() GSVA package: may see, detected presence duplicated gene set identifiers applied deduplication policy, can changed parameter dedupUse; see help page ?readGMT deduplication policies. getGmt(), also takes parameter geneIdType, default automatically detect type gene identifier fill corresponding metadata resulting GeneSetCollection object, principle need specify .
code: library(GSEABase)  URL <- paste("https://data.broadinstitute.org/gsea-msigdb/msigdb/release",              "2023.2.Hs/c7.immunesigdb.v2023.2.Hs.symbols.gmt", sep="/") genesets <- getGmt(URL, geneIdType=SymbolIdentifier()) genesets #> GeneSetCollection #>   names: GOLDRATH_EFF_VS_MEMORY_CD8_TCELL_DN, GOLDRATH_EFF_VS_MEMORY_CD8_TCELL_UP, ..., KAECH_NAIVE_VS_MEMORY_CD8_TCELL_UP (4872 total) #>   unique identifiers: ABCA2, ABCC5, ..., LINC00841 (20457 total) #>   types in collection: #>     geneIdType: SymbolIdentifier (1 total) #>     collectionType: NullCollection (1 total) length(genesets) #> [1] 4872 genesets.list <- geneIds(genesets) head(lapply(genesets.list, head, 3), 3) #> $GOLDRATH_EFF_VS_MEMORY_CD8_TCELL_DN #> [1] "ABCA2"   "ABCC5"   "ABHD14A" #>  #> $GOLDRATH_EFF_VS_MEMORY_CD8_TCELL_UP #> [1] "ADAM8" "AK3"   "ANLN"  #>  #> $GOLDRATH_NAIVE_VS_EFF_CD8_TCELL_DN #> [1] "ACOT7" "ADAM8" "ANLN" URL <- paste("https://data.broadinstitute.org/gsea-msigdb/msigdb/release",              "7.5/c2.all.v7.5.symbols.gmt", sep="/") genesets <- getGmt(URL, geneIdType=SymbolIdentifier()) #> Error in validObject(.Object): invalid class "GeneSetCollection" object: each setName must be distinct library(GSVA)  genesets <- readGMT(URL) #> Warning in deduplicateGmtLines(lines, deduplUse): GMT contains duplicated gene #> set names; deduplicated using method: first genesets #> GeneSetCollection #>   names: CORONEL_RFX7_DIRECT_TARGETS_UP, FOROUTAN_TGFB_EMT_UP, ..., SA_TRKA_RECEPTOR (6363 total) #>   unique identifiers: ABAT, DIP2A, ..., IGHG3 (21728 total) #>   types in collection: #>     geneIdType: SymbolIdentifier (1 total) #>     collectionType: NullCollection (1 total)

text: Introduction GSEABase vignette GSEABase package. help pages getGmt() readGMT() functions GSEABase GSVA packages, respectively. Bioconductor package msigdb provides API directly querying downloading MSigDB GMT files GeneSetCollection objects. Similarly msigdb, msigdbr CRAN package provides API query download MSigDB GMT files, tidy data.frame objects one gene per row.

title: How to read paired-end reads from a BAM file
text: illustration, use data pasillaBamSubset data package. Paired-end reads can loaded GenomicAlignments::readGAlignmentPairs GenomicAlignments::readGAlignmentsList functions GenomicAlignments package. functions use mate pairing algorithm output different objects. Let’s start GenomicAlignments::readGAlignmentPairs: GAlignmentPairs class holds pairs; reads mate ambiguous pairing discarded. list element holds exactly 2 records (mated pair). Records can accessed first last segments template left right alignments. See ?GAlignmentPairs GenomicAlignments package information. readGAlignmentsList, mate pairing performed asMates set TRUE BamFile object, otherwise records treated single-end. GAlignmentsList general ‘list-like’ structure holds mate pairs well nonmates (.e., singletons, records unmapped mates etc). mates_status metadata column (accessed mcols) indicates records paired. Non-mated reads returned groups QNAME contain number records. non-mate groups range size 1 9.

title: How to read single-end reads from a BAM file
text: illustration, use data pasillaBamSubset data package. Several functions available reading BAM files R: GenomicAlignments::readGAlignments() GenomicAlignments::readGAlignmentPairs() GenomicAlignments::readGAlignmentsList() Rsamtools::scanBam() scanBam low-level function returns list lists discussed . See ?scanBam Rsamtools package information. Single-end reads can read readGAlignments function GenomicAlignments package. Data subsets can specified genomic position, field names, flag criteria using Rsamtools::ScanBamParam. , example import records overlap position 1 5000 negative strand chromosome 4, flag cigar metadata columns. Another approach subsetting data use Rsamtools::filterBam. function creates new BAM file records passing user-defined criteria. See ?filterBam Rsamtools package information.

what: How to retrieve a gene model from AnnotationHub
title: How to retrieve a gene model from AnnotationHub
text: gene model available GRanges GRangesList object data package, may available AnnotationHub. HOWTO, look gene model Drosophila melanogaster AnnotationHub. Create `hub’ filter Drosophila melanogaster: 457 files match Drosophila melanogaster. look metadata hub, can see 5th record represents GRanges object UCSC RefSeq Gene track. can retrieve dm3 file GRanges like : metadata fields contain details file origin content. Split GRanges object gene name get GRangesList object transcript ranges grouped gene. can now use txbygn summarizeOverlaps function prepare table read counts RNA-Seq differential gene expression.

what: How to manipulate GRanges objects with tidy principles
title: How to use tidy principles for genomic ranges manipulation
text: illustration, going download xlsx file provided Supplementary Data (Serizay et al. 2020), manipulate using tidy principles. Good practices recommended Bioconductor consortium suggest use BiocFileCache package download cache files remote servers. Excel files can read tibbles R thanks read_xlsx() function readxl package. convert GRanges object directly using as_granges() plyranges package, using tidy evaluation dplyr package. Let’s say interested metadata columns, want keep annot_dev_age_tissues, Annotation, columns ending _RPM. can use select() function dplyr select columns, just like data.frame tibble. fact, verbs dplyr package can used GRanges objects, filter(), mutate(), arrange(), group_by() summarise(). Note summarize() function automatically returns DataFrame, GRanges object. enhance compatibility tidyverse ecosystem, plyranges package provides as_tibble() method GRanges objects, converts tibbles. allows use pass GRanges objects ggplot visualization.  Finally, join concept introduced dplyr package can also applied GRanges objects. case, operation refers joining overlapping, nearest, preceding/following ranges, using join_*_*() function plyranges package. example, can compare annotations (Serizay et al. 2020) chromosome arms/center

what: How to manipulate SummarizedExperiment objects with tidy principles
title: How to use tidy principles for RNA-seq data analysis
text: illustration, use data airway package. airway object printed controlled tidySummarizedExperiment package, default displayed tibble-like object. Note behavior can changed setting options(restore_SummarizedExperiment_show = TRUE) option. airway object SummarizedExperiment object, tidySummarizedExperiment packages adds invisible layer abstract SE object tibble. allows us use dplyr package manipulate data, eventually directly plug data flow ggplot2 visualization.  tidy methods defined SummarizedExperiment objects, classes built top SummarizedExperiment, DESeqDataSet SingleCellExperiment, can also manipulated tidy principles. prompted development packages (included tidyomics meta-package) extend tidy principles analyses, single-cell transcriptomics analysis.

title: A Collection of How To Documents For Bioconductor
text: Fork repository create new branch. Copy rename howto_template.qmd file inst/templates vignettes directory, edit copy accordingly. Please choose name new vignette way unique clearly indicates content. Note title vignette added title field %\VignetteIndexEntry YAML section. Test vignette locally fresh R session, make sure self-contained runs without errors. Add name Author list DESCRIPTION file. HowTo using packages already included among dependencies BiocHowTo, please add list Imports DESCRIPTION file. One way via usethis package: Add new vignette suitable section ‘Articles’ _pkgdown.yml file. Push changes forked repository open pull request devel branch parent repository. submissions reviewed Bioconductor Training Committee.
